results.title: UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf
results.author: Dryad Global
results.text:
UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

[Maritime Risk Intelligence Blog](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/)

# UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

Written by [Dryad Global](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/author/dryad-global)| March 1, 2026 at 8:00 PM

On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.

This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.

Claims of closure or transit restr
highlights:
On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.
[...]
This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.
[...]
Claims of closure or transit restrictions in the Strait of Hormuz continue to circulate via open source reporting and VHF, but no official closure has been communicated through recognized channels such as NAVAREA warnings or IMO broadcasts; such VHF indications do not constitute a legal suspension of transit passage under UNCLOS. Broader regional instability extends threats beyond the Strait of Hormuz, including elevated tensions in the Bab al Mandab. JMIC guidance urges continuous VHF Channel 16 watch, AIS transmission per policy, adherence to Traffic Separation Schemes and corridors, professional responses to hails, enhanced risk assessments (considering military activity and insurance), and immediate reporting of unusual activity, suspicious approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or other centers. The situation is under close monitoring with further updates expected as warranted.
results.summary:
Summary:
- UKMTO reports a highly volatile security environment across the Strait of Hormuz and adjacent waters (Hormuz, Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab) driven by ongoing regional military activity.
- Key risks for watchstanders: increased potential for miscalculation/misidentification near military units and critical facilities; disruptive VHF communications, targeted hails, and GNSS/electronic interference (AIS, positioning, navigation, comms) that may be intermittent.
- No official closure of the Strait of Hormuz has been announced; circulating reports of transit restrictions should not be treated as UNCLOS-specified suspensions.
- JMIC guidance for watchstanders: maintain continuous VHF Channel 16 watch, comply with AIS policy and Traffic Separation Schemes, respond professionally to hails, perform enhanced risk assessments (considering military activity and insurance implications), and promptly report unusual activity, suspicious approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or other centers.
- Implication for Strait of Hormuz watchstanding: heightened vigilance, robust communications discipline, strict adherence to traffic regimes, and proactive reporting to manage risks from military activity and potential electronic disruptions. Further updates likely as the situation develops.

results.title: Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News
results.text:
Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News

# Iran's Maritime Risk Increases; Strait of Hormuz Traffic Near Halt Amid Advisories

US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.

Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.

## US MARAD Issues Advisory 2026-004 on March 16

The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with
highlights:
US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.
[...]
Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.
[...]
The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with coalition queries, and coordinate transits with NAVCENT NCAGS. The advisory warns against complying with over-the-horizon VHF or email instructions from Iranian forces, advising vessels to log encounters and continue on track.
[...]
The Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC), via UKMTO, elevated the maritime threat level to critical across the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. More than 20 incidents have occurred since February 28 escalations, including attacks on vessels, navigation system interference, and risks to port infrastructure. The risk of further disruptions remains high.
[...]
UKMTO has reported over 20 maritime incidents in and around the Strait since March 1. Operators are urged to monitor VHF 16, report abnormal activity, and stay aware of electronic interference and misidentification risks.
[...]
Commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains extremely limited. On March 15, only three outbound crossings were recorded, with no inbound transits, marking continued suppression far below normal levels. Maritime activity across the Gulf stayed heavily disrupted, distorting cargo flows and energy shipments.
[...]
Since the conflict's onset, 20 confirmed incidents targeted commercial vessels and offshore infrastructure, primarily cargo ships: 40 percent bulk carriers, 30 percent tankers, 20 percent container vessels, and 10 percent service vessels. About 45 percent of affected vessels had recent UAE port calls, with others from Iraq, Thailand, Vietnam, and Brazil.
[...]
, MARAD, and flag directives. Dual-theater risks combine Hormuz with Red Sea exposures, prompting route redist
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz and surrounding Persian Gulf are currently at elevated maritime security risk (March 2026), with authorities flagging ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping and high likelihood of further incidents.
- Key advisories:
  - US MARAD Advisory 2026-004 (Mar 16): High risk to commercial shipping; maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units; coordinate with NAVCENT NCAGS; log encounters; avoid over-the-horizon instructions; GNSS spoofing/jamming persists.
  - UKMTO/JMIC: Threat level raised to critical; monitor VHF Channel 16; report abnormal activity; be aware of electronic interference and misidentification risks.
- Traffic impact:
  - Transit volumes through the Strait of Hormuz are severely depressed; as of mid-March, outbound crossings were minimal with virtually no inbound transits.
  - Since late February, over 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities have been confirmed, including attacks, navigation-system interference, and threats to port infrastructure.
- Operational guidance for watchstanders:
  - Maintain heightened situational awareness and maintain distance from naval units when transiting; coordinate with NCAGS for planned routes and timings.
  - Exercise extreme caution with GNSS-dependent navigation and be prepared for spoofing/jamming events.
  - Monitor official advisories, report suspicious activity, and avoid reliance on unverified over-the-horizon instructions.
  - Prepare for possible port restrictions or delays at regional hubs (e.g., UAE ports) and anticipate debris, airspace restrictions, and potential mine threats.
- Overall: For watchstanders, prioritize risk-aware routing in the Strait of Hormuz/ Gulf of Oman, continuous intelligence updates, and strict adherence to liaison procedures with naval and coalition authorities to mitigate attack, spoofing, and misidentification risks.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

FILE PHOTO: Tankers are seen off the coast of the Fujairah, as Iran vows to close the Strait of Hormuz, amid the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Amr Alfiky/File Photo

# Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to [rattle the region](https://gcaptain.com/tag/iran-conflict/), according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).

In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.

While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapse
highlights:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to rattle the region, according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).
[...]
In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.
[...]
While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapsed as shipowners and charterers reassess the risks of transiting one of the world’s most important maritime chokepoints.A review of vessel tracking data cited in the advisory shows that traffic through the waterway has nearly stopped.
[...]
“Recent AIS review indicates transits have reduced to single-digit levels, with only one confirmed commercial transit observed in the past 24 hours,” the advisory said.
[...]
Data from MarineTraffic.com shows tanker traffic through Strait of Hormuz down by more than 90%. “Unlike several other vessel segments where movements have largely ceased, some tankers are still traveling east and west through the strait, with a number of voyages occurring under AIS blackouts,” says Matt Wright, Principal Freight Analyst at Kpler, parent company to MarineTraffic.com.
[...]
A containership transiting the Strait
[...]
region. The
[...]
is one of
[...]
across the region in
[...]
The JMIC advisory also highlighted widespread GPS and GNSS interference affecting vessels operating in the region, including AIS anomalies, positional inaccuracies, and degraded communications systems. Shipmasters were urged to closely monitor navigation equipment and be prepared to rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation techniques if satellite-based systems become unreliable.
[...]
that the maritime security environment could
[...]
missile activity expands closer to major shipping routes or if strikes occur against ports, offshore infrastructure
[...]
or terminals supporting Gulf energy exports
[...]
Shipping companies operating in the region have been advised to conduct enhanced voyage risk assessments before entering the Gulf, maintain reporting links with the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) center and the Maritime Security Centre – Indian Ocean (MSCIO), and follow established Best Management Practices for maritime security.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains an extremely volatile maritime security environment amid U.S.-Israel-Iran tensions.
- A Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) advisory deems the risk to merchant shipping “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is almost certain, with traffic in the strait near standstill.
- Regular commercial transits have collapsed to single-digit levels; about 90% reduction in tanker traffic, though the strait is not formally closed.
- Incidents include missile/drone attacks and at least one projectile strike on a containership (Safeen Prestige), underscoring elevated threat to vessels and crews.
- GNSS/GPS interference and AIS anomalies are widespread, degrading navigation systems. Masters should be prepared to rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation if satellite systems fail.
- The strait remains globally critical for energy, handling roughly one-fifth of world oil consumption and a significant LNG export flow, heightening the impact of security disruptions on supply chains.
- Operational guidance for watchstanders: monitor navigation equipment closely, be ready to switch to alternative navigation techniques, anticipate heightened threat levels, and maintain heightened readiness for rapid route planning and risk mitigation.

results.title: Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?
results.author: Sky News      Tue, 10 March 2026 at 3:14 pm CET   5 min read
results.text:
Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?

# Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?

Iran's move to shut the Strait of Hormuz has brought shipping through the 24-mile stretch of water close to a standstill.

Only a handful of vessels have transited in recent days, many switching off their tracking systems or linked to "shadow fleets".

"They can't physically close a waterway of that size, but the threat is there", said Richard Meade, editor-in-chief of maritime risk company Lloyd's List Intelligence.

Mr Meade said Iran has a proven history of using everything from ballistic missiles to unmanned aerial and seaborne drones.

What is making it through?

Sky News' Data & Forensics team has focused on 13 ships that have transited the Strait of Hormuz between 2 and 9 March.

We know the real number of crossings is much higher, as some ships turn their tracking system off, a practice known as going "dark".

Data from marine analytics firm IMF Portwatch suggests that in normal times,

[...]

On 2 March, the data shows the ship did an almost U-turn at around 2pm UTC. It continued to retreat from the area until 4 March, finally returning to the Port of Salalah in Oman, heading to the Gulf of Kutch on 10 March, where it appeared "laded", heavily loaded.

Mr Meade said: "We're seeing a number of ships doing U-turns. They're likely getting orders to go and perform alternative operations. Now, that's fine for the ships that are headed into the Gulf, but for the ships that are already there, they're essentially stuck."
results.summary:
Summary:
The Strait of Hormuz remains a high-risk chokepoint as Iran’s moves to shut the waterway have reduced traffic and heightened security concerns. Key points for watchstanders:

- Traffic disruption: Crossing activity has dropped, with many ships turning on-off AIS (“dark”) to avoid tracking, signaling increased risk and reduced situational awareness.
- Shadow fleet activity: A notable portion of crossings (at least 8 of 13 identified) involve “shadow fleet” vessels carrying sanctioned cargo linked to Iran, Russia, or Venezuela, complicating enforcement and risk assessment.
- Regional and global vessels involved: Crossings include ships from Greece, India, UAE, Singapore, the US, Marshall Islands, and others, indicating a broad international security concern and the need for vigilant, expanded surveillance.
- Attack incidents: At least 17 incidents affecting vessels were reported in late February to March, including fires and suspected projectile strikes, underscoring threats to shipping and the necessity for heightened shipboard and port security procedures.
- Responses and guidance: Authorities (UKMTO and allied maritime risk groups) emphasize continuous threat monitoring, rapid threat reporting, and readiness to execute emergency procedures in case of further attacks or disruptions.

Operational implications for watchstanders:
- Increase AIS monitoring for “dark” transits and corroborate with multiple data sources to maintain convoy and traffic awareness.
- Prioritize incident response readiness and interdictive reporting for suspected attacks or unusual activity near the strait.
- Coordinate with international partners to track shadow fleet movements and assess risk to sanctioned cargo routes.
- Maintain updated security advisories and ensure crew training on boarding, damage control, and emergency communications in case of projectile or other attack events.

results.title: Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain

Oil tankers pass through the Strait of Hormuz, December 21, 2018. REUTERS/Hamad I Mohammed

# Strait of Hormuz Enters Active Crisis: Five Ships Hit, Traffic Collapses, Tanker Markets Brace for Historic Shock

The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.

As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has [elevated](https://www.ukmto.org/-/media/ukmto/products/update-002---001---jmic-advisory-note-28_feb_2026_final.pdf?rev=54f3ebd757e14bccb7e6ac9b62108bcb) the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

In just 24 hours, five vessels w

The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.
[...]
As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has elevated the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

Stand-off missile and

- Jebel Ali briefly suspended operations following an aerial interception that caused a fire before resuming limited activity.
- Duqm Port in Oman remains operational after two UAV strikes injured one worker.
- Ras Tanura Refinery in Saudi Arabia was reportedly shut following a drone strike and fire.
- Bahrain temporarily suspended port operations and pilotage services.
- Qatar’s Ras Laffan and Mesaieed ports reported significant GPS signal degradation.
[...]
Significant GNSS/GPS interference is being reported across the Strait’s approaches, compounding navigational risks amid heavy congestion and heightened aerial threats.
[...]
AIS tracking indicates transits through the Strait may have fallen to just 28 vessels over a 24-hour period — roughly an 80% decline from the historical daily average near 138 vessels.
[...]
Cargo transits dropped from 98 combined eastbound/westbound movements on February 28 to just 18 on March 1. Tanker movements fell even more sharply, from 50 combined transits to just three.
[...]
JMIC cautioned the drop may reflect a short-term pause rather than a structural halt, but insurers and operators are clearly reassessing risk.
[...]
“Insurance availability may now act as a primary gating factor for transit decisions independent of formal navigational closure,” JMIC noted.
[...]
Even absent an official blockade, the combination of aerial threat, port suspensions, insurance withdrawals, and GPS disruption is effectively constraining commercialadfad movement.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz has entered an active crisis with concentrated missile and drone attacks on five vessels in the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters, elevating regional maritime security risk to CRITICAL per JMIC. Expect near‑term instability in vessel transits and heightened surveillance requirements for watchstanders.
- Operational impact for watchstanders:
  - Vigilance on all merchant traffic: “All vessels at risk” regardless of flag, with a broad threat surface spanning commercial ships, tankers, and support vessels.
  - AIS and GNSS/GPS interference: Significant GNSS disruption complicates navigation and tracking; plan for degraded positioning and reliance on alternative navigation aids and collision avoidance protocols.
  - Port/energy infrastructure risk: Intermittent outages or delays at Jebel Ali, Ras Tanura, Ras Laffan, Duqm, Bahrain ports, and related facilities indicate shifting risk to critical maritime infrastructure, with potential impacts on routing and transit planning.
  - Stand-off threats persist: Ongoing missile/drone risks require continuous threat assessment, risk mitigation, and readiness to reroute or hold at safe harbor if advised.
- Vessel-specific notes: Attacks impacted crude tanker MKD VYOM (fire, one casualty), SKYLIGHT (injuries, crew evacuated), STENA IMPERATIVE (hit while berthed; worker fatalities), HERCULES STAR (fire contained), and near-miss for OCEAN ELECTRA. No unified operational linkage identified among targets, but the risk posture is broad and dynamic.
- Watchstanding guidance:
  - Maintain heightened AIS vigilance and cross-check with regional advisories (JMIC/UK-MTO) for evolving threat levels.
  - Implement robust cyber/GNSS contingency planning; prepare alternate navigation and communications routines.
  - Anticipate possible port advisories, repair outages, and redirection recommendations; monitor insurance and war-risk coverage developments.
  - Coordinate with naval/military authorities and fleet operations to share threat intel and adjust routes and speed to minimize exposure.

If you want, I can tailor this to a specific watchteam role (bridge, operations, risk, or security) or convert it into a briefing slide outline.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation
results.author: Dimitra Sotirchos
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global

Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.

At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.

Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.

Pole Star Global fuses AIS, satellite detections, ownership,
highlights:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global
[...]
Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.
[...]
At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.
[...]
Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.
[...]
Pole Star Global
[...]
analytics to help governments
[...]
from manipulation. Our data offers an unusually
[...]
how conflict,
[...]
, and digital
[...]
are reshaping global trade in
[...]
The image below shows intentional AIS manipulation by a vessel called the NV Aquamarine (in red trails). This vessel was travelling at an astounding 102 knots (188 km/hr) from the Strait to the Iran/Iraq border. This is impossible for a tanker that has a capacity to carry 350,000 barrels of oil worth $35M at current spot rates.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is experiencing systematic GPS spoofing and AIS manipulation, with about 1 in 15 vessels showing suspicious positional anomalies (roughly 231 of 3,396 tracked). Typical signs include implausible jumps, brief ghosting, and reappearances that indicate deliberate interference rather than random glitches.
- Commercial traffic through the strait has largely halted, while some vessels outside the chokepoint broadcast unusual destinations (e.g., “China Owner and All Crew”), signaling political messaging and potential coercion.
- A large pool of oil tankers (about 540 ships carrying ~314 million barrels) remain at sea with no fixed destination, logged as “awaiting orders.” This creates a de facto floating oil exchange worth tens of billions, highlighting vulnerability to disruption or manipulation.
- Pole Star Global combines AIS, satellite detections, ownership, and behavioral analytics to help governments, insurers, and shipping companies distinguish malfunction from deliberate manipulation, enabling better risk assessment and decision-making in real time.
- Notable example: the NV Aquamarine showed AIS manipulation with speeds up to 102 knots (188 km/h), an impossibility for a tanker, illustrating how such interference can distort situational awareness and potentially escalate security risks.
- Overall relevance to watchstanding: maritime security teams should treat AIS/GPS anomalies as potential indicators of coercion or conflict shaping, monitor for abnormal traffic patterns and destinations, assess the risk to critical commodities (oil flows), and incorporate cross-checks (satellite detections, ownership, behavior analytics) to distinguish technical faults from malicious activity. Proactive watchstanding should emphasize containment (secure routing, alternative corridors), threat intelligence integration, and readiness to respond to intentional disruption in a high-stakes chokepoint.

results.title: Cargo vessel catches fire in Strait of Hormuz after projectile hit, UKMTO says | Iran International
results.text:
Cargo vessel catches fire in Strait of Hormuz after projectile hit, UKMTO says | Iran International

# Cargo vessel catches fire in Strait of Hormuz after projectile hit, UKMTO says
Mar 11, 2026, 06:00 GMT
Listen to this article
Share
A cargo vessel was hit by an unidentified projectile in the Strait of Hormuz, causing a fire on board and prompting the crew to evacuate, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said on Wednesday.
The vessel requested assistance after the strike, UKMTO said.
The agency said earlier in the day that it had received a report of an incident 25 nautical miles northwest of Ras Al Khaimah in the United Arab Emirates. The master of a container vessel said the ship had sustained damage from a suspected but unidentified projectile.
UKMTO said the extent of the damage was under investigation and that all crew members were safe.
### Most Viewed
[
![Iranian expats say UAE canceled their residency visas, leaving many stranded](https://i.iranintl.com/images/rdk9umy0/production/3d9f2d63443f06de2e3ecf6e7f3daf47757ee0c1-992x661.png?w=576&h=384&q=80&fit=max&auto=format)
1

highlights:
A cargo vessel was hit by an unidentified projectile in the Strait of Hormuz, causing a fire on board and prompting the crew to evacuate, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said on Wednesday.
[...]
The vessel requested assistance after the strike, UKMTO said.
[...]
The agency said earlier in the day that it had received a report of an incident 25 nautical miles northwest of Ras Al Khaimah in the United Arab Emirates. The master of a container vessel said the ship had sustained damage from a suspected but unidentified projectile.

[...]

[...]
Before traffic through Hormuz was disrupted, roughly 20 million barrels per day of oil and petroleum products passed through the corridor, most of it bound for Asia. In addition, about 330 million cubic meters of liquefied gas moved through the same route daily.
[...]
Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates have diverted part of their exports through alternative pipeline routes, but those volumes remain well below what previously moved through the strait.
[...]
Energy consultancies including Wood Mackenzie and Kpler warned early that global markets could withstand severe volatility for only three to four weeks without a reopening of the waterway.
[...]
The strategic stakes are becoming increasingly explicit. On Monday night, President Donald Trump warned that the United States would respond “20 times harder” if shipping through Hormuz were not restored.
[...]
On Tuesday, Iran’s security chief Ali Larijani responded on X with a defiant message: “The Strait of Hormuz will either be a strait of peace and prosperity for all or will be a strait of defeat and suffering for warmongers,” adding, “beware lest you be the ones to vanish.”
[...]
on price volatility
[...]
on physical supply
[...]
strategic reserves and
[...]
"And as soon as the Straits of Hormuz can be opened and guaranteed, we're going to see the prices drop back further. The main issue here is that we have to collapse this regime. Otherwise the issue of oil prices is going to be far more acute, because this country will have nuclear weapons and ballistic missiles, which they can
[...]
to hold the entire world hostage. That isn't going to be going to defeat this regime. We're going to stop the proliferation
results.summary:
Summary:
- A cargo vessel in the Strait of Hormuz was struck by an unidentified projectile, igniting a fire on board. The crew evacuated and later reported all crewSAFE; the incident is under investigation.
- UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) confirmed the attack and said the vessel requested assistance. The strike occurred about 25 nautical miles northwest of Ras Al Khaimah (UAE) and damage details are being assessed.
- Relevance to maritime security: This event highlights ongoing risks to commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for global energy flows. It underscores the need for enhanced situational awareness, rapid distress signaling, and coordinated international response to ensure crew safety and maritime security in high-tension corridors.

results.title: Iran starts to formalise its chokehold on Hormuz with a 'toll booth' regime | World News - Business Standard
results.author: Business Standard
results.text:
Iran starts to formalise its chokehold on Hormuz with a 'toll booth' regime | World News - Business Standard

[Home](https://www.business-standard.com/)/ [World News](https://www.business-standard.com/world-news)/ Iran starts to formalise its chokehold on Hormuz with a 'toll booth' regime

# Iran starts to formalise its chokehold on Hormuz with a 'toll booth' regime

## Only about 150 vessels, including tankers and container ships, have transited since March 1, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence shipping information firm

(Photo: PTI)

[AP](https://www.business-standard.com/author/ap) Frankfurt 

5 min read Last Updated : Mar 27 2026 | 6:56 PM IST

#### Listen to This Article

Iran appears to be setting itself up as the gatekeeper for the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most important artery for oil shipments. The move could cement Tehran's de facto chokehold over the crucial waterway and formalize its ability to keep its own oil flowing to China.

Iranian communications to the United Nations maritime authority and the experience of ships transiting the strait suggest the creation of something akin to a "toll booth." Ships must enter Iranian waters and be vetted by Iran's Islamic
highlights:
Iran appears to be setting itself up as the gatekeeper for the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most important artery for oil shipments. The move could cement Tehran's de facto chokehold over the crucial waterway and formalize its ability to keep its own oil flowing to China.
[...]
Iranian communications to the United Nations maritime authority and the experience of ships transiting the strait suggest the creation of something akin to a "toll booth." Ships must enter Iranian waters and be vetted by Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps. At least two vessels have paid for passage.
[...]
About half of the vessels turn off radio identification systems that show their location before going through, and reappear on the other side in the Gulf of Oman. There's a reason for their reluctance and caution. At least 18 ships have been hit and at least seven crew members have been killed, according to the U.N.'s International Maritime Organization, which tracks maritime security. It did not specify which nation attacked the vessels.
[...]
Lloyd's List says tolls
[...]
toll booth' regime in
[...]
Strait of Hormuz
[...]
shipping information firm Lloyd
[...]
s List Intelligence.
[...]
Normally ships use a two-lane shipping channel in the middle of the strait. But increasingly, vessels are taking a different route, to the north around Larak Island, placing them in Iran's territorial waters and closer to the Iranian coastline.
[...]
Entities that want their vessels to safely pass through must submit their details to what Lloyd's List Intelligence refers to as "approved intermediaries" of the Revolutionary Guard, including the cargo, owners, destination and a complete crew list. Approved vessels receive a code and are escorted by an IRGC vessel. Oil is prioritized and vessels are subject to "geopolitical vetting," Lloyd's said.
[...]
Iran appears to be setting up a permanent system On Tuesday, the IMO received a letter from the Iranian government saying it "had implemented a set of precautionary measures aimed at preserving maritime safety and security." The letter claimed Iran was acting within the principles of international law.
[...]
The Fars and Tasnim news agencies, both close to Iran's Revolutionary Guard, quoted lawmaker Mohammadreza Rezaei Kouchi saying "parliament is pursuing a plan to formally codify Iran's sovereignty, control and oversight over the Strait of Hormuz, while also creating a source of revenue through the collection of fees." The IMO has condemned the attacks on vessels and called for an internationally coordinated approach to secure passage through the strait that respects freedom of navigation.
results.summary:
Summary:
- Iran appears to formalize a regulatory “toll booth” regime for the Strait of Hormuz, effectively giving Tehran a gatekeeper role over passage.
- Ships entering Iranian waters must be vetted by Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) and pass through approved intermediaries; tolls are reportedly paid in yuan.
- Since March, only about 150 vessels have transited, a fraction of typical traffic, with a notable shift of activity toward Iran-aligned routes and vessels.
- Key security implications for watchstanders:
  - Increased IRGC involvement in routing, vetting, and escorting vessels raises compliance and risk management requirements for ships transiting Hormuz.
  - A de facto control mechanism over passage could be used to leverage or disrupt flows, potentially affecting insurance, crew safety, and emergency response planning.
  - Reduced transits and irregular AIS practices (radio silence) heighten security vigilance for potential misrepresentation, staging of threats, or escalation scenarios.
  - The majority of recent traffic has shifted eastward through Gulf routes; however, Iran-associated ships have accounted for a rising share (up to ~60% early in the conflict, recently ~90% in certain periods), underscoring Iran’s leverage in the corridor.
- Watchstanding considerations:
  - Monitor for official channeling of passage via IRGC intermediaries and any changes to tolling structures, documentation, or escort procedures.
  - Track vessel vetting codes, convoy escorts, and any deviations from the standard two-lane channel, including movements near Iran’s territorial waters.
  - Maintain situational awareness of security incidents in the Strait and adjacent waters, as the toll regime could influence risk levels and response coordination.
  - Be aware of potential price and routing pressures, especially for Chinese and other non-U.S.-sanctioned trade, and how that affects shipping confidence and insurance costs.
- Overall: The development signals a shift toward formalized Iranian control over Hormuz transit, with significant implications for maritime security watchstanding, risk assessment, and contingency planning in the Strait of Hormuz corridor.

results.title: The Hormuz shipping crisis in numbers | AGBI
results.author: John Crowley
results.text:
The Hormuz shipping crisis in numbers | AGBI

# The Hormuz shipping crisis in numbers

April 1, 2026, 4:38 AM

Reuters/Laban Walloga

A container ship diverted from the Gulf to Lamu Port in Kenya in mid-March. Thousands of vessels are stuck in the Strait of Hormuz

The Strait of Hormuz has become a floating patchwork of cargos, registries and declared identities – but in [war](https://www.agbi.com/topics/iran-israel-conflict/), what a ship claims to be may be up for negotiation.

Data shared with AGBI by maritime intelligence company Pole Star Global captures 3,208 vessels still signalling their presence in and around the chokepoint on March 20.

Thousands of bulk carriers, crude and oil products tankers, [container ships](https://www.agbi.com/logistics/2026/03/container-lines-reroute-away-from-iran-war-to-india-and-sri-lanka/), tugs and general cargo have clustered in the Persian Gulf.

Together, they represent billions of dollars’ worth of commodities, including [chemicals](https://www.agbi.com/industry/2026/03/qatari-lng-shutdown-puts-11-of-global-helium-supply-at-risk/), [metals](https://www.agbi.com/industry/2026/03/sulphur-market-faces-demand-destruction-as-war-disrupts-suppl
highlights:
A container ship diverted from the Gulf to Lamu Port in Kenya in mid-March. Thousands of vessels are stuck in the Strait of Hormuz
[...]
The Strait of Hormuz has become a floating patchwork of cargos, registries and declared identities – but in war, what a ship claims to be may be up for negotiation.
[...]
Data shared with AGBI by maritime intelligence company Pole Star Global captures 3,208 vessels still signalling their presence in and around the chokepoint on March 20.
[...]
Thousands of bulk carriers, crude and oil products tankers, container ships, tugs and general cargo have clustered in the Persian Gulf.
[...]
These graphics break down the fleet by type and status sailing under some of the world’s best-known registries, including Panama, Liberia, the Marshall Islands and the UAE.
[...]
It resembles a League of Nations at sea. But in the conflict environment, that comes with a warning label. These charts show vessels with their automatic identification system (AIS) switched on – transponders that broadcast their position, identity, course and destination.
[...]
Yet the system is only as reliable as the data being fed into it. Iran’s so-called shadow fleet, for example, often sails off-grid.
[...]
Visibility can degrade in an instant, according to Pole Star Global.
[...]
Saleem Khan, the company’s chief data and analytics officer, told AGBI that around one in 10 vessels operating in the Gulf are showing “anomalous” AIS behaviour, including spoofing, electronic interference and transponder deactivation.
[...]
The same caution applies to the flag on the stern. It does not reflect ownership, Pole Star said. A vessel registered in Panama or Singapore may be owned, controlled or commercially linked to interests elsewhere in the world.
[...]
Khan said many vessels were now in effect sailing with “forged passports at sea”.
[...]
The risks are not just commercial. Very large crude carriers typically move at 13 knots (15mph) through the chokepoint, forcing crews and operators to weigh whether to risk the contested waterway at all.
[...]
Pole Star Global says there have been at least 29 incidents in the Gulf, from ships being sunk to vessels set ablaze. At least a dozen merchant mariners have been killed.
[...]
On March 31 Iran attacked a fully loaded crude oil tanker off Dubai. Trying to navigate the 21-mile stretch of water between Iran and Oman has left crews, in Khan’s words, as “sitting ducks”.
[...]
### Iran conflict dents traffic at Oman’s busiest airport
results.summary:
Summary:

- The Strait of Hormuz is highly congested and opaque, with about 3,208 vessels present around March 20, spanning bulkers, tankers, container ships, tugs, and general cargo.
- AIS data is unreliable in the conflict environment: roughly 10% of Gulf ships show anomalous AIS behavior (spoofing, interference, or transponder deactivation), and many ships use forged or misleading flags and voyage details.
- Ship ownership/registry can be misleading: flags of convenience and ownership often diverge, complicating traceability and risk assessment.
- Malicious or deceptive tactics are evident: some vessels broadcast altered destinations or “friendly” owner signals to pass through or avoid scrutiny, signaling potential adversarial manipulation.
- Operational risk drivers for watchstanding:
  - High visibility of “real-time” ship positions can be distorted, reducing situational awareness.
  - The presence of a “shadow fleet” operating off-grid increases uncertainty about ownership, destination, and intent.
  - High-speed transit limits decision windows for risk-averse routing or detours (typical speeds around 13 knots for VLCCs/ICCs heighten time pressure in a contested chokepoint).
- Implications for maritime security watchstanders:
  - Prioritize corroborating AIS with additional data sources (satellite, radar, merchant registry checks) to separate legitimate traffic from spoofed or misrepresented signals.
  - Be vigilant for forged or mid-voyage-destination changes indicating possible attempts to gain passage through sensitive corridors.
  - Recognize that flag state does not reliably indicate ownership/control; consider beneficial ownership, charters, and industry links in threat assessments.
  - Monitor for swarm-like clustering and concentration of assets near the Hormuz chokepoint, which can complicate routing decisions and increase collision or incident risk.
  - Prepare for rapid visibility degradation and data anomalies; maintain contingency watch routines and escalation paths when data quality deteriorates.

Key takeaways for watchstanders: treat AIS-based visibility as indicative rather than definitive, validate with multiple data sources, and account for misinformation or deception tactics that could be used to gain passage or obscure true intent around the Strait of Hormuz.

results.title: Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge | Global Maritime Hub
results.author: Editor
results.text:
Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge | Global Maritime Hub 

- [Login](https://globalmaritimehub.com/my-account)
- [Shipping Trends](https://globalmaritimehub.com/category/shipping-trends)

# Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge

- [Editor](https://globalmaritimehub.com/author/david)
- March 12, 2026

Container ships around the Strait of Hormuz are experiencing unprecedented AIS signal disruptions, according to industry analysts Alphaliner.

Alphaliner live satellite vessel tracking shows that 48 container ships in the waters west of the Strait of Hormuz currently have their transponders switched off or are broadcasting spoofed positions, as of 10 March.

Disruption in the area reached even higher levels earlier in the month. A record 65 container ships registered lost or manipulated signals on 4 March, when the number of vessels with visible and disrupted transmissions was split exactly in half.

Abnormally high levels of signal disruption in the region persisted over the following days, with 62 ships recorded on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, and 60 on 8 March.

For comparison, during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, the
highlights:
Container ships stranded near Strait of
[...]
Container ships around the Strait of Hormuz are experiencing unprecedented AIS signal disruptions, according to industry analysts Alphaliner.
[...]
Alphaliner live satellite vessel tracking shows that 48 container ships in the waters west of the Strait of Hormuz currently have their transponders switched off or are broadcasting spoofed positions, as of 10 March.
[...]
Disruption in the area reached even higher levels earlier in the month. A record 65 container ships registered lost or manipulated signals on 4 March, when the number of vessels with visible and disrupted transmissions was split exactly in half.
[...]
Abnormally high levels of signal disruption in the region persisted over the following days, with 62 ships recorded on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, and 60 on 8 March.
[...]
For comparison, during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, the number of container ships around Hormuz operating with disrupted signals peaked at 32 vessels.
[...]
The last container ship recorded leaving the Persian (Arabian) Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz was observed on 5 March, and was a sub-3,000 TEU vessel. Since then, no additional container ships have exited the Gulf.
[...]
In total, 131 container ships remain stranded west of the Strait of Hormuz, according to live vessel monitoring.
[...]
### Strait of Horm
[...]
cargo bookings resume for Gulf states
[...]
### Strait of Hormuz cargo bookings resume for Gulf states
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is experiencing unprecedented AIS (Automatic Identification System) disruptions, with 60–65% of recent vessel activity affected as ships broadcast either off or spoofed positions. At peak on March 4, 65 ships showed disrupted signals; by March 10, 48 ships were impacted, leaving 131 container ships stranded west of Hormuz.
- The disruption signals a potential security concern for maritime watchstanders: reduced situational awareness and increased risk of misnavigation, spoofing, and possible escalation if threats exploit the communication gaps.
- Historical context shows similar, though lesser, disruption during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War (peaking at 32 ships). The current event surpasses that level, indicating a significant anomaly in the region’s electronic navigation and tracking environment.
- Operational implications for watchstanders:
  - Heightened vigilance for anomalous vessel behavior, potential spoofing, and sudden course changes near the strait.
  - Increased reliance on corroborating sources (radar, visual observation, merchant vessel reports) due to AIS signal degradation.
  - Priority attention to traffic density, standby response planning for potential blockages or standoffs, and coordination with regional authorities and AIS data providers.
  - Potential impact on safe entry/exit of vessels through Hormuz, with cascading effects on global shipping and oil transit security.
- The event underscores the need for robust cyber-physical security measures in the Strait of Hormuz, including redundant navigation/positioning data streams, hardened AIS protocols, and enhanced watchstanding protocols during periods of signal disruption.

results.title: Iran warning near Hormuz collides with Red Sea shipping comeback - AL-Monitor: The Middle Eastʼs leading independent since 2012
results.text:
Iran warning near Hormuz collides with Red Sea shipping comeback - AL-Monitor: The Middle Eastʼs leading independent since 2012 

# Iran warning near Hormuz collides with Red Sea shipping comeback

## Armed boats attempted to intercept a vessel near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, the same day that major container lines also announced the resumption of limited transits through the Suez Canal.

[Samuel Wendel](https://www.al-monitor.com/authors/samuel-wendel)

Feb 3, 2026

Ships sail along Egypt's Suez Canal near Ismailia on April 16, 2025. — KHALED DESOUKI/AFP via Getty Images

- [عربي](https://www.al-monitor.com/ar/originals/2026/02/thdhyr-ayrany-qrb-mdyq-hrmz-ytzamn-m-wdt-hrkt-almlaht-fy-albhr-alahmr)
- [简体中文](https://www.al-monitor.com/zh-hans/originals/2026/02/yilangzaihuoermuzifujinfachudejinggaoyuhonghaihangyunfusufashengchongtu)
- [Español](https://www.al-monitor.com/es/originals/2026/02/la-advertencia-de-iran-cerca-de-ormuz-choca-con-el-regreso-del-transporte)

Fears of renewed disruption to Middle Eastern shipping routes resurfaced Tuesday after armed boats attempted to [intercept](https://www.al-monitor.com/originals/2026/02/iranian-boats-approach-us-flagged-tanker-strai
highlights:
## Armed boats attempted to intercept a vessel near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, the same day that major container lines also announced the resumption of limited transits through the Suez Canal.
[...]
Fears of renewed disruption to Middle Eastern shipping routes resurfaced Tuesday after armed boats attempted to intercept a US-flagged tanker near the Strait of Hormuz— the same day that major container lines Hapag-Lloyd and Maersk also announced the resumption of limited transits through the Suez Canal.
[...]
Details: A group of armed small boats attempted to intercept a vessel roughly 16 nautical miles north of Oman on Tuesday, according to the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations. Per Reuters, Iranian gunboats approached the US-flagged Stena Imperative and ordered the tanker to stop its engine.
[...]
UKMTO said the vessel was hailed over radio by several armed vessels but ignored requests to stop and continued on its planned route. The incident occurred in the inbound traffic lane near the mouth of the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway linking the Gulf to the Indian Ocean. Authorities are investigating and UKMTO advised ships to transit the area with caution. Early on Tuesday, just hours before, a US F-35C fighter jet shot down an Iranian drone near an American aircraft carrier in the Indian Ocean.
[...]
and follows several ship
[...]
around the strategic waterway. On Jan.
[...]
, Iran’s Press TV reported
[...]
planning live-fire exercises in the straits off the
[...]
’s southern
[...]
on Feb.
[...]
official later refuted the news.
[...]
with Palestinians in Gaza
[...]
a ceasefire last
[...]
While Tuesday’s Hormuz incident appears, as of this writing, to have been a warning rather than a major escalation, it reinforces how Iran can signal pressure without shutting the waterway — a move widely seen as a last resort that would also harm Tehran’s own oil exports. As of 1:53 p.m. EST on Tuesday, oil prices had risen on the news, with benchmark Brent crude trading above $67 per barrel, up about 1.5%.
[...]
Yet the intertwined risks remain. In January, the Iran-aligned Houthis hinted they could resume attacks on shipping if US-Iran tensions worsen, injecting fresh uncertainty just as operators test the waters. That linkage means any escalation around Hormuz could quickly undermine confidence in the Red Sea recovery, reversing hard-won gains for global trade while threatening more economic pain for Egypt, which relies on Suez Canal revenues.
results.summary:
Summary:
- A near-Hormuz incident saw armed boats approach a US-flagged tanker (Stena Imperative) about 16 nautical miles north of Oman, signaling ongoing maritime security risks in the Strait of Hormuz amid elevated US-Iran tensions.
- The event followed reports of Iran’s IRGC activity and drone-related confrontations near US assets in the Indian Ocean, highlighting the region’s volatility and its impact on commercial shipping.
- Concurrent developments: major container lines (Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd) announced a cautious return of limited transits through the Suez Canal and Red Sea under naval escort, indicating a fragile restoration of Red Sea–Mediterranean trade routes.
- Implications for watchstanding: heightened demand for vigilant vessel security in the Hormuz traffic lanes, adherence to UKMTO advisories, readiness to respond to radio hailings, and coordination with naval escorts and regional partners to mitigate disruption risks to critical oil and general cargo flows.

results.title: BERNAMA - More Ships Reportedly Pass Through Strait Of Hormuz
results.text:
BERNAMA - More Ships Reportedly Pass Through Strait Of Hormuz

WORLD

# More Ships Reportedly Pass Through Strait Of Hormuz

03/04/2026 10:07 PM

FILE PHOTO: Cargo ships in the Gulf, near the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from northern Ras al-Khaimah, near the border with Oman’s Musandam governance, amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, in United Arab Emirates, March 11, 2026. REUTERS/Stringer/File Photo/File Photo

CAIRO, April 3 (Bernama-dpa) -- The number of ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz has recently increased, German Press Agency (dpa) reported, citing a report by data firm Windward on Friday.

Windward said 16 cargo ships passed through the largely blocked strait on Wednesday, compared with 11 the previous day. The number of passages has now risen for three consecutive days.

However, it still amounts to only a fraction of the traffic prior to the Iran conflict, which began at the end of February when Israel and the United States launched attacks on Tehran.

Iran has responded by blocking traffic through the key waterway, which is critical to the global oil and gas trade.

Windward said most of the ships passing in recent days sailed close to the Iranian coast alo
highlights:
FILE PHOTO: Cargo ships in the Gulf, near the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from northern Ras al-Khaimah, near the border with Oman’s Musandam governance, amid the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, in United Arab Emirates, March 11, 2026. REUTERS/Stringer/File Photo/File Photo
[...]
CAIRO, April 3 (Bernama-dpa) -- The number of ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz has recently increased, German Press Agency (dpa) reported, citing a report by data firm Windward on Friday.
[...]
Windward said 16 cargo ships passed through the largely blocked strait on Wednesday, compared with 11 the previous day. The number of passages has now risen for three consecutive days.
[...]
However, it still amounts to only a fraction of the traffic prior to the Iran conflict, which began at the end of February when Israel and the United States launched attacks on Tehran.
[...]
Iran has responded by blocking traffic through the key waterway, which is critical to the global oil and gas trade.
[...]
Windward said most of the ships passing in recent days sailed close to the Iranian coast along the island of Larak, allowing Iran to maintain a "permission-based, selective blockade."
[...]
The report stated that three ships also passed through the strait close to the Omani coast, avoiding the corridor controlled by Iran.
[...]
One of the ships, the liquefied natural gas (LNG) tanker Sohar, has arrived off the shore of Muscat, vessel tracking data from MarineTraffic showed.
[...]
It is said to be the first LNG tanker to have passed through the strait since the outbreak of the war.
[...]
A container ship belonging to the French shipping giant CMA also passed through the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday to leave the Persian Gulf, the French broadcaster RTL reported. The ship’s navigation signal reportedly indicated a "French owner."

results.summary:
Summary:
- Recent reports indicate a modest uptick in shipping through the Strait of Hormuz amid ongoing Iran-Israel-U.S. tensions. Windward data shows 16 cargo ships passed through on one day, up from 11 the day before, marking a three-day rise but still far below pre-conflict traffic.
- Iran has responded by enforcing a selective, permission-based blockade, focusing passages near its coast, particularly along the island of Larak. Some vessels have bypassed Iran-controlled corridors by moving closer to the Omani coast.
- Notable instances include:
  - LNG tanker Sohar passing through and arriving off Muscat, reportedly the first LNG transit since the conflict began.
  - A CMA CGM container ship traversing the strait to exit the Persian Gulf, with signals indicating a French ownership.
- Implications for watchstanding:
  - The strait remains a high-risk chokepoint with fluctuating traffic tied to political-military actions, necessitating close monitoring of convoy movements, coastwise corridors, and blockade patterns.
  - Watchstanders should track vessel routing near Larak and Oman’s approaches, identify deviations from the Iran-controlled corridor, and monitor LNG/transit signals for changes in risk posture and potential escalation.
